one_line_takeaway: Prompting an LLM to predict, as its own internal safety classifier, the exact harmful response it would flag as unsafe reliably extracts that harmful content -- and the more accurate a model's own safety judgment is, the more exploitable it becomes (Bayes-theorem-derived B -> 1 as safety awareness J -> infinity), a relationship the authors verify empirically (Pearson R=0.80 across 30 models) and causally (GRPO safety-awareness manipulation on 4 models).
key_finding: Safety-judgment accuracy and Posterior ASR are strongly positively correlated across 30 open-source models (Pearson R=0.80, p=1.32e-07; Spearman=0.78, p=3.63e-07): weaker/older models (Llama 2 family, Llama 3.2 1B/3B, Qwen3 0.6B) cluster below 65% safety-judgment accuracy and near-zero ASR, while frontier open models (Qwen 3.6, Gemma 4) exceed 85% accuracy with ASR surging past 80% toward ~100%. On frontier LLMs, Posterior Attack achieves an average ASR of 83.0% across the 7 evaluated models, beating the strongest baseline CodeChameleon (71.9% average) and exceeding ReNeLLM/FlipAttack by nearly 40 points; per-model ASRs from the paper's Table 2 include GPT-4o 78.7%, GPT-5-Chat 99.0%, GPT-5 33.7%, Claude 3.7 Sonnet 78.3%, Claude Sonnet 4.6 93.7%, Qwen3 235B 98.5%, DeepSeek V3.2 99.2%. Notably, scaling GPT-4o to GPT-5-Chat drove CodeChameleon down 36.7 points and ReNeLLM down 42.9 points, while Posterior Attack rose from 78.7% to 99.0% (+20.3 points); Claude 3.7->4.6 dropped CodeChameleon from 94.0% to 30.4% and neutralized FlipAttack (60.8%->0.0%) while Posterior ASR rose from 78.3% to 93.7%. The GRPO causal experiments (Table 1) show, e.g., Llama-3.1-8B: SAI raised HB-CLS by 2.5 points and Posterior ASR by 9.2 points (81.1%->90.3%); SAD lowered HB-CLS by 8.4 points and collapsed Posterior ASR from 81.1% to 24.4% (a 56.7-point drop). Falcon3-7B: SAI +7.2 HB-CLS points drove +11.6 ASR points; Qwen3-1.7B: +0.5 HB-CLS -> +4.8 ASR; Qwen2.5-3B: +0.5 HB-CLS -> +1.8 ASR -- with GSM8K/MMLU/Direct-ASR fluctuations bounded to a maximum of 0.9%/0.8%, supporting a causal (not merely correlational) link. Cost analysis: Posterior Attack uses ~3,300 input / ~4,000 output tokens and ~$0.03 per query, versus e.g. ReNeLLM's ~15,000 output tokens/$0.23 per query and ManyShot's ~75,000 input tokens. Test-time scaling (reasoning effort) partially defends GPT-OSS/GPT-5 (e.g., GPT-OSS-20B ASR drops 98.7% -> 91.2% (low) -> 6.9% (medium) -> 0.2% (high); GPT-OSS-120B reaches 0.0% at high effort; GPT-5 drops from 99.0% baseline to 33.7% at low reasoning effort but only to ~51.5% at medium/high) but is ineffective or counterproductive elsewhere: Claude Sonnet 4.6 stays 93.7-96.2% ASR regardless of effort while using only ~120 reasoning tokens; Qwen 3.6 35B's thinking mode raises ASR from 43.5% to 95.2% despite >2,400 reasoning tokens.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Posterior Attack, a single-query, search-free jailbreak that reframes the attack surface from asking an LLM to directly produce harmful content (maximizing the prior P(Y=1|X)) to asking the model, acting as its own safety classifier, to generate the exact response that its internal safety judge would flag as unsafe (simulating the posterior P(Y=1|X,Z=1)). Because the model is nominally performing an 'objective classification task' rather than being asked to comply with a harmful request, it produces the harmful content anyway. The authors then formalize and empirically/causally validate the 'Safety Paradox': using Bayes' theorem they derive that Posterior ASR (B) = Jp/(Jp+(1-p)), where p is the base attack success rate and J = TPR/FPR is a 'Safety Awareness' term (the positive likelihood ratio of the model's own safety classification); B is proven monotonically increasing in both p and J, and B -> 1 as J -> infinity, meaning a model that is a near-perfect safety classifier is maximally exploitable by this specific attack. This is the paper's own theoretical/analytical contribution, distinct from the cited mechanistic-interpretability work (Arditi et al. 2024 refusal directions, Wei et al. 2024 / Zhao et al. 2025 safety neurons, Li and Kim 2025 explicit safety-signal steering) which the paper explicitly says treated internal safety classification as a purely defensive asset -- the paper's contribution is to weaponize it and formally explain why.
threat_model: Single-query, black-box, no gradient access and no fine-tuning required by the attacker. The attacker crafts one prompt that casts the target model as a 'red teaming classifier' being asked which generation would make it answer 'Yes' to whether behavior X is unsafe (Table 7 posterior prompt template); the model, in the course of computing this classification, generates the harmful content itself. Applies to both open-weight models (30 evaluated, up to 35B parameters: LLaMA, Qwen, Falcon, Gemma, Mistral families) and closed frontier systems accessed via API (GPT-4o, GPT-5, GPT-5-Chat, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Qwen3 235B, DeepSeek V3.2). The paper explicitly scopes out proprietary system-level guardrails that sit outside the model's completion logic (noted as a limitation) and is conducted only on English-language models/datasets.
limitations: Paper's own stated limitations: (1) ASR is computed via LLM judges (HarmBench LLaMA-2-13B, GPT-4o-mini) which the authors report have ~90% agreement with human experts (Appendix B) but could still introduce measurement error. (2) Evaluation is scoped to AdvBench and models accessible as of early 2026; proprietary system-level guardrails operating outside the model's core completion logic are not fully captured. (3) Study is English-language only; cross-lingual/cross-cultural generalization of the Safety Paradox is untested. (4) Deliberative Alignment (test-time reasoning) is identified as the most promising defense but its high computational latency currently limits feasibility for high-throughput, real-time applications, and the paper notes it is 'not impenetrable' even in GPT-5.

datasets_used: AdvBench (Zou et al. 2023) - 520 harmful behaviors, used for ASR computation
HarmBench (Mazeika et al. 2024) - 596 harmful/benign queries, used to measure models' own safety-judgment/classification accuracy (HB-CLS)
WildGuardTrain (Han et al. 2024) - source of 4,096 balanced (prompt, response) pairs for GRPO fine-tuning interventions
GSM8K (Cobbe et al. 2021) - general utility check post-intervention
MMLU (Hendrycks et al. 2021) - general utility check post-intervention
quantitative_results: Safety-judgment accuracy vs Posterior ASR correlation across 30 open-source LLMs: Pearson R=0.80 (p=1.32e-07), Spearman=0.78 (p=3.63e-07)
Frontier model average ASR: PosteriorAttack 83.0% vs best baseline CodeChameleon 71.9%
Per-model Posterior ASR (Table 2): GPT-4o 78.7%, GPT-5-Chat 99.0%, GPT-5 33.7%, Claude 3.7 Sonnet 78.3%, Claude Sonnet 4.6 93.7%, Qwen3 235B 98.5%, DeepSeek V3.2 99.2%

GRPO causal intervention (Table 1): Llama-3.1-8B SAI: HB-CLS +2.5 -> Posterior ASR +9.2 (to 90.3%); Llama-3.1-8B SAD: HB-CLS -8.4 -> Posterior ASR -56.7 (81.1%->24.4%); Falcon3-7B SAI: HB-CLS +7.2 -> Posterior ASR +11.6; Qwen3-1.7B SAI: HB-CLS +0.5 -> Posterior ASR +4.8; Qwen2.5-3B SAI: HB-CLS +0.5 -> Posterior ASR +1.8; utility (GSM8K/MMLU) and Direct ASR fluctuations bounded to max 0.9%/0.8%
Cost per query: Posterior Attack ~$0.03 (~3,300 input / ~4,000 output tokens) vs ReNeLLM ~$0.23 (~15,000 output tokens) vs ManyShot ~75,000 input tokens vs DeepInception ~13,000 output tokens

Test-time reasoning-effort ablation: GPT-OSS-20B ASR 98.7% (none) -> 91.2% (low,55 tok) -> 6.9% (medium,484 tok) -> 0.2% (high,3,843 tok); GPT-OSS-120B 0.0% ASR at high effort; GPT-5 99.0% baseline -> 33.7% (low,595 tok) -> ~51.5% (medium/high); Claude Sonnet 4.6 stays 93.7-96.2% ASR across all effort levels (~120 reasoning tokens); Qwen 3.6 35B thinking mode raises ASR 43.5% -> 95.2%; Gemma 4 26B thinking reduces ASR 98.8% -> 76.3% using >1,600 reasoning tokens
